title: Boulangère Potatoes
subtitle: Sliced potatoes baked in stock under the roast
excerpt: Boulangère potatoes are thinly sliced potatoes and onions baked in stock in a single dish. They absorb the liquid as they cook, becoming tender, savoury, and lightly golden at the edges. The name comes from the old French custom of sending the dish to the baker's oven after the morning's bread was done. They are the classic accompaniment to a roast leg of lamb, placed beneath the joint to catch the dripping fat.
sourceNotes: Classic French boulangère method, widely documented in British and French cookery from the nineteenth century. Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): boulangère potatoes with roast lamb. Delia Smith, Complete Cookery Course (1978): method and ratios.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to 2 hours ahead and refrigerate. Add 10 minutes to the cooking time if going in from cold.

glossaryTerms.definition: A French cooking term meaning 'in the style of the baker's wife': dishes slow-baked in an oven, traditionally taken to the local bakery after the bread baking was done to make use of residual heat.

content.text: Boulangère potatoes are a simpler relative of gratin dauphinois: no cream, no cheese, just potatoes, onions, and stock. The
method produces a less rich result that pairs better with a large roast, where the potato's job is to absorb the dripping fat and complement rather than compete with the meat.

Thin, even slicing is important: a mandoline gives 2 mm slices that cook evenly; a knife gives 3 to 4 mm slices that take a little longer. A floury potato (Maris Piper, King Edward) absorbs the stock and softens into the layers; a waxy variety stays firmer and does not absorb as well.

items.prepNote: Maris Piper, approx. 1.2 kg, peeled and thinly sliced
for dotting over the top layer

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Butter a roasting dish or large ovenproof pan. Layer alternating potato slices and onion rings, seasoning each layer with {{sea-salt-flakes}}, {{black-pepper}}, thyme leaves, and tucking in the bay leaves as you go. Arrange the top layer of potatoes neatly overlapping. Dot {{unsalted-butter}} of butter over the surface.

Pour {{stock-chicken}} of hot stock over the layered potatoes. The stock should come to just below the top layer. Bake for 80 to 90 minutes until the potatoes are completely tender when pierced with a knife and the top layer is golden-brown.

rows.symptom: Stock absorbed but potatoes still undercooked
rows.cause: Slices were too thick, or stock ran out before the potatoes softened
rows.fix: Add 100 ml more stock or water, cover with foil, and continue for a further 20 minutes.

rows.symptom: Top layer burning before lower layers are cooked
rows.cause: Oven too hot or dish too shallow
rows.fix: Cover with foil for the first 45 minutes and remove for the final 30 to allow the top to colour.

rows.symptom: Layers separate and slide when served
rows.cause: Not enough cooking time for the starch to bind the layers
rows.fix: Allow to rest in the dish for 10 minutes before cutting. The starch firms up as it cools slightly.

content.text: Boulangère potatoes are the standard French accompaniment to a leg of lamb, cooked in the same oven with the leg sitting on top for the final hour, the lamb fat basting the potato surface. This arrangement makes excellent practical sense: one oven, one dish, the potato taking on the flavour of the roast above it. In a domestic context without a large boulangerie oven, the dish is simply baked alongside the joint.
